summary: Hotz responds to Curtis Yarvin's liberal/conservative heroin/cocaine analogy, embracing the 'heroin' side as a self-identified progressive who believes in technological inevitability. He argues that technology only moves forward, that trying to reverse progress would require god-like power, and that humanity's eventual death is thermodynamically guaranteed anyway. He dismisses AI doomer concerns as overblown thought experiments while warning that 'don't build AI' is a dumb strategy more likely to cause civilizational regression. The post concludes with pragmatic nihilism: build the future not because it matters, but because you can.
key_points: Conservatives inevitably lose because time and technology only move forward - reversing progress would require technology more powerful than the current state
Humanity will die eventually due to entropy; this is basic physics, not speculation
Paperclipping AI scenarios are unrealistic because the competitive search processes that create AI won't stop at any single outcome
Overly cautious moves like 'don't build AI' are more dangerous than building, risking civilizational dark ages
The correct stance is pragmatic building without indefinite optimism - create because you can, not because it ultimately matters
money_quotes: Liberals sell heroin, conservatives sell cocaine.
I am not a conservative. Conservatives lose. If you want to not lose, you have to stop being a conservative.
The best thing you can do to make SF better is leave.
Technology is a one-way street. Ironically, it would take immensely powerful technology to try to drive in reverse on that street.
Humanity is going to die. Is this news to you? Did you pay attention in high school physics?
A paperclipping AI makes no sense beyond a thought experiment. The search processes that created that AI will not stop at paperclipping!
Don't build AI is a really dumb strategy.
Build the world you want to see tomorrow. Not because it matters, but because we can. And what the hell else are you doing?
key_insight: Technology's forward march is inevitable and trying to stop it is both futile and more dangerous than accelerating it, so build not out of optimism but because there's nothing better to do.
